citations.narrative:
The licensee has not been maintaining medical records for the two dogs. The licensee stated that the dogs have received
their physical exams and are current on vaccinations and routine testing, however, she was not able to produce records of
the preventive medical procedures during the inspection.
Medical records for dogs are important to ensure that the dogs are receiving adequate veterinary care in a timely and
appropriate manner and to verify that the facility is following the Program of Veterinary Care as directed by the attending
veterinarian.
Exhibitors must keep copies of medical records for dogs which include the identity of the dog, routine husbandry
(vaccinations, preventive medical procedures, or treatments), and findings for tests or examinations. Correct by
maintaining medical records for all regulated dogs.
Correct by: 03/09/2026.

An outdoor enclosure housing a Flemish Giant rabbit has a large gap where the wire has become detached from the
wood structure. The gap, approximately 4"x 3" in size, has resulted from the wire, on the side of the enclosure, being
pulled away from the securing staples and has created numerous sharp points in the enclosure.
Failure to maintain rabbit enclosures can allow for gaps or sharp points which can result in injury, entrapment, escape of
the animals or access for predators.
All primary enclosures for rabbits shall be structurally sound and maintained in good repair to protect the rabbits from
injury, to contain them and to keep predators out. Correct by securing the wire to eliminate the gap and sharp points.
Correct by: 02/16/2026.

An outdoor enclosure (#8) housing one rabbit, has a large accumulation of feces on the wire flooring. The feces is
approximately 1-2" deep and covers approximately half of the flooring space in the wire part of the enclosure.
Large accumulations of excreta can expose the rabbits to disease hazards, create odors, and attract pests.
Correct by ensuring that primary enclosures for rabbits are kept reasonably free of excreta, hair cobwebs and other debris
by periodic cleaning.
Correct by: 02/16/2026.

An enclosure housing four young chinchillas is not being maintained in good repair. The elevated wire resting platform
has two areas where the wires are broken creating holes and several sharp points.
Failure to maintain enclosures can allow for holes, unstable structures, and sharp points which can result in injury or
entrapment of the animals.
Facilities shall be maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury and contain them. Correct by removing,
repairing, or replacing the broken wire areas.
Correct by: 02/16/2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee.
n

narrative:
3.13(b) 
   
Veterinary care for dogs. 
The licensee has not been maintaining medical records for the two dogs. The licensee stated that the dogs have received 
their physical exams and are current on vaccinations and routine testing, however, she was not able to produce records of 
the preventive medical procedures during the inspection.  
Medical records for dogs are important to ensure that the dogs are receiving adequate veterinary care in a timely and 
appropriate manner and to verify that the facility is following the Program of Veterinary Care as directed by the attending 
veterinarian. 
Exhibitors must keep copies of medical records for dogs which include the identity of the dog, routine husbandry 
(vaccinations, preventive medical procedures, or treatments), and findings for tests or examinations. Correct by 
maintaining medical records for all regulated dogs.  
Correct by: 03/09/2026. 
3.53(a)(1) 
   
Primary enclosures. 
An outdoor enclosure housing a Flemish Giant rabbit has a large gap where the wire has become detached from the 
wood structure. The gap, approximately 4"x 3" in size, has resulted from the wire, on the side of the enclosure, being 
pulled away from the securing staples and has created numerous sharp points in the enclosure.  
Failure to maintain rabbit enclosures can allow for gaps or sharp points which can result in injury, entrapment, escape of 
the animals or access for predators. 
All primary enclosures for rabbits shall be structurally sound and maintained in good repair to protect the rabbits from 
injury, to contain them and to keep predators out. Correct by securing the wire to eliminate the gap and sharp points.  
Correct by: 02/16/2026. 
3.56(a) 
   
Sanitation. 
An outdoor enclosure (#8) housing one rabbit, has a large accumulation of feces on the wire flooring. The feces is 
approximately 1-2" deep and covers approximately half of the flooring space in the wire part of the enclosure.  
Large accumulations of excreta can expose the rabbits to disease hazards, create odors, and attract pests. 
Correct by ensuring that primary enclosures for rabbits are kept reasonably free of excreta, hair cobwebs and other debris

by periodic cleaning.  
Correct by: 02/16/2026. 
3.125(a) 
   
Facilities, general. 
An enclosure housing four young chinchillas is not being maintained in good repair. The elevated wire resting platform 
has two areas where the wires are broken creating holes and several sharp points. 
Failure to maintain enclosures can allow for holes, unstable structures, and sharp points which can result in injury or 
entrapment of the animals. 
Facilities shall be maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury and contain them. Correct by removing, 
repairing, or replacing the broken wire areas. 
Correct by: 02/16/2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee.
